abstract:
Many Americans express a blend of conservative and liberal views across different policy issues. While traditional research characterizes these voters as cross-pressured, an influential study, “Moderates” (Fowler et al. 2023), argues that they actually hold centrist views on individual issues. To support this, Fowler et al. develop a mixture model designed to identify voters whose preferences are well-summarized by a single liberal-conservative dimension. Because the model classifies the vast majority of voters as ideological "Downsians," the authors conclude that those with centrist estimated scores must be "genuine moderates" who support middle-of-the-road policies.

In this article, we show that this method systematically overstates the prevalence of ideological consistency. The "Moderates" framework defaults to classifying voters as ideological unless they either answer questions randomly or belong to a single, specific cluster of non-ideologues with a shared set of idiosyncratic views. Through simulations and analysis of the original data, we demonstrate that this approach frequently mischaracterizes cross-pressured voters as moderate ideologues simply because their mixed responses do not fit the narrow definition of the model's non-ideological category. Consequently, the conclusion that these voters support centrist policy remains in doubt. We suggest that distinguishing between genuine centrist views and idiosyncratic issue mixes requires richer data—specifically panel studies and more granular policy scales—rather than the interpretation of binary cross-sectional survey responses.
